_meta.authoringNotes: Citations cross-verified against ACG 2019 (Rubin, PMID 30840605), AGA 2020 (Feuerstein, PMID 31816372), ECCO 2022 (Raine, PMID 35040476), and pivotal RCTs: OCTAVE (Sandborn 2017, PMID 28467869), TRUE NORTH (Sands 2021, PMID 34081380), ELEVATE UC (Danese 2022, PMID 35644166), LUCENT (Sandborn 2023, PMID 37133584). Global epidemiology from Ng et al. Lancet 2017 (PMID 29050646). Doses and monitoring align with STRIDE-II treat-to-target targets.

h1Title: Ulcerative Colitis: Symptoms, Causes, Diagnosis, and Treatment
heroOverview: Ulcerative colitis is a chronic immune-mediated inflammatory bowel disease that produces continuous mucosal ulceration of the colon, starting at the rectum and extending proximally without skipping segments. It affects roughly 1.2 million people in the United States and around 5 million worldwide (Ng Lancet 2017), with peak incidence in North America and Europe and rising rates across newly industrialized regions. The hallmark presentation is bloody diarrhea with urgency and tenesmus that persists for weeks before diagnosis. Untreated disease drives anemia, weight loss, hospitalization for severe flares, and a measurable lifetime risk of colorectal cancer that begins eight years after diagnosis. Modern care is built on a treat-to-target principle: induce remission with 5-aminosalicylates, corticosteroids, or advanced therapies (biologics, JAK inhibitors, S1P modulators), then maintain endoscopic healing for life.

keyStats.prevalence: ~1.2 million US adults; ~5 million worldwide (Ng Lancet 2017, PMID 29050646)
keyStats.demographics: Slight male predominance; highest incidence in North America (19-23/100,000/year) and Northern Europe (24/100,000/year)
keyStats.avgAge: Bimodal onset — main peak age 15-35, second smaller peak age 50-70
keyStats.globalCases: Doubled in newly industrialized countries over the past 30 years

definition: Ulcerative colitis (ICD-10: K51) is a chronic relapsing-remitting inflammatory bowel disease characterized by continuous mucosal and submucosal inflammation that begins in the rectum and extends proximally to a variable degree, always limited to the colon. The histologic signature is crypt distortion, crypt abscesses, basal plasmacytosis, and depleted goblet cells, with inflammation confined to the mucosa rather than penetrating the full bowel wall as in Crohn's disease. The pathogenesis is multifactorial — genetically susceptible hosts (over 200 risk loci identified, with NOD2 less prominent than in Crohn's) develop dysregulated mucosal immunity against the gut microbiome, driven by Th2 and Th9 pathways and a leaky epithelial barrier. Disease extent is described by the Montreal classification as proctitis (E1, rectum only), left-sided colitis (E2, up to the splenic flexure), or extensive colitis / pancolitis (E3, beyond the splenic flexure), and severity is graded by Mayo score, UCEIS endoscopic score, or the Truelove-Witts criteria for acute severe disease. Ulcerative colitis is managed by gastroenterologists with multidisciplinary input from colorectal surgeons, dietitians, and ophthalmology, dermatology, and rheumatology when extraintestinal manifestations occur.

typesClassification.description: Inflammation limited to the rectum. Roughly 30-40% of patients at diagnosis. Bleeding and urgency dominate; systemic features are uncommon. Often responds to topical mesalamine alone.

Inflammation extending from the rectum to the splenic flexure. Roughly 30-40% of patients. Bloody diarrhea, urgency, and cramping are typical, and combined oral plus topical 5-ASA is the usual first-line.

Inflammation proximal to the splenic flexure, often involving the entire colon. Roughly 20-30% of patients. Higher risk of severe flares, hospitalization, colectomy, and long-term colorectal cancer surveillance.

Defined by Truelove-Witts criteria (>6 bloody stools per day plus one of: pulse >90, temperature >37.8 C, hemoglobin <10.5 g/dL, ESR >30). Requires hospital admission, IV corticosteroids, and structured rescue planning at day 3-5.
Disease that fails to enter steroid-free remission or relapses on steroid taper. Triggers escalation to biologic, small molecule, or surgical management.

primarySymptoms: Bloody diarrhea, often with visible red blood mixed into stool or coating it, persisting for weeks rather than days and present in roughly 90% of patients at presentation.
Urgent need to defecate (urgency), with little warning between the urge and the need to reach a toilet, frequently disrupting work, travel, and sleep.
Tenesmus — a painful, persistent sensation of incomplete rectal emptying with straining even after passing stool, especially prominent in proctitis.
Increased stool frequency, ranging from 4-6 loose stools daily in moderate disease to more than 10 stools per day with overt blood in severe flares.
Mucus or pus discharge per rectum, sometimes passed alone without stool, reflecting active rectal inflammation.
Lower abdominal cramping pain that improves transiently after passing stool, typically localized to the left lower quadrant in left-sided disease.
Nocturnal bowel movements that wake the patient from sleep — a useful clinical clue that separates inflammatory diarrhea from functional disorders like irritable bowel syndrome.
Fatigue and reduced exercise tolerance, often disproportionate to bowel symptoms and driven by chronic inflammation, anemia, and disturbed sleep.
Unintentional weight loss and reduced appetite in moderate-to-severe disease, particularly during extended flares.
Low-grade fever, tachycardia, and pallor in severe flares, with frank malaise that signals impending acute severe colitis requiring admission.

Extraintestinal symptoms — peripheral arthritis, axial spondyloarthritis, episcleritis or uveitis, erythema nodosum, pyoderma gangrenosum, and pruritus or jaundice from primary sclerosing cholangitis (PSC, ~5%).
earlyWarningSigns: Bloody streaks on stool or on toilet paper persisting for more than two weeks, especially in a person under 40 without hemorrhoids
New onset of urgent, frequent loose stools with overnight bowel movements
Iron-deficiency anemia found on routine bloods with no obvious source of bleeding
Recurrent mouth ulcers, joint pain, or skin nodules with persistent loose stools
Persistently elevated fecal calprotectin (above 150-250 µg/g) on screening despite resolution of any infection
emergencySigns: More than six bloody stools per day with tachycardia (>90 bpm), fever (>37.8 C), anemia (hemoglobin <10.5 g/dL), or ESR >30 — Truelove-Witts criteria for acute severe ulcerative colitis (ASUC), which warrants same-day hospital admission
Sudden abdominal distension with severe pain, reduced bowel sounds, and systemic illness — concerning for toxic megacolon or perforation
Lightheadedness, syncope, or large-volume bright red blood in the toilet — significant hemorrhage requiring resuscitation and urgent endoscopic assessment
New severe calf swelling or sudden breathlessness during a UC flare — venous thromboembolism risk is roughly three-fold elevated in active disease
Severe right upper quadrant pain, jaundice, or worsening pruritus — possible cholangitis on a background of primary sclerosing cholangitis

causes.description: Genome-wide association studies have identified over 200 inflammatory bowel disease risk loci, with HLA class II variants, IL23R, and barrier-function genes among the strongest UC signals. First-degree relatives carry a 3-15% lifetime risk, far above the population baseline.

An exaggerated Th2 and Th9 cytokine response, with secondary involvement of IL-13 and IL-23, sustains inflammation against luminal microbial antigens. Regulatory T-cell function is impaired, allowing chronic activation rather than resolution.

Patients show reduced diversity, loss of short-chain fatty acid producers such as Faecalibacterium prausnitzii, and expansion of mucolytic and pro-inflammatory species. Whether dysbiosis triggers UC or is a consequence remains contested.
Goblet cell depletion, defective mucin (MUC2) production, and tight-junction abnormalities allow luminal antigens to access the lamina propria, perpetuating immune activation.

Western diet patterns high in ultra-processed foods, antibiotic exposure (especially in childhood), urban living, and improved hygiene are consistently associated with rising UC incidence in epidemiologic studies (Ng Lancet 2017).

Active smoking is associated with lower UC risk and milder disease — the opposite of Crohn's disease. Ex-smokers experience increased flare risk in the years after quitting. The mechanism likely involves nicotine effects on mucus production and immune signaling. This is descriptive only — patients should not start or resume smoking, as the cardiovascular and cancer risks outweigh any UC benefit.
Appendectomy before age 20 for true appendicitis is consistently associated with lower UC risk in cohort studies, suggesting an appendix-related immunologic mechanism.

riskFactors.factor: Family history of inflammatory bowel disease
riskFactors.description: First-degree relatives of UC patients have a 3-15% lifetime risk, compared with around 0.3% in the general population. Risk is highest with an affected sibling.
Incidence follows a bimodal distribution with a main peak in young adulthood and a smaller second peak after age 50.
Prevalence is roughly two to four times higher than in other ethnic groups, reflecting enrichment of IBD risk variants.
Diets high in ultra-processed foods, refined sugar, emulsifiers, and red meat correlate with higher UC incidence in cohort data; Mediterranean-style eating is associated with lower risk.
Regular non-selective NSAID exposure is linked to UC flares and may unmask disease, likely via prostaglandin-mediated effects on mucosal repair.
Salmonella, Campylobacter, or C. difficile gastroenteritis is associated with a roughly two-fold increase in subsequent IBD diagnosis within 12 months.
Incidence is higher in urban areas and at higher latitudes, with vitamin D status and microbiome differences proposed as mediators.
Ex-smokers carry higher UC risk than current smokers; relapse risk increases in the first year after quitting. This does not justify continuing tobacco, given competing harms.
Multiple courses of broad-spectrum antibiotics in the first years of life are associated with modestly elevated IBD risk, plausibly through microbiome disruption.
Modest increase in UC risk observed in large cohort studies; clinical relevance is small but worth mentioning in shared decision-making.

affectedDemographics: US prevalence: approximately 1.2 million adults; Canadian provinces report some of the highest global rates (~300 per 100,000)
Northern European incidence: 19-24 per 100,000 person-years, the highest globally
Asia-Pacific incidence has risen 3-5 fold over three decades as countries industrialize (Ng Lancet 2017)
Jewish populations, particularly Ashkenazi, have prevalence 2-4 times higher than non-Jewish populations
Slight male predominance overall, more pronounced in late-onset disease
diagnosisOverview: Diagnosis of ulcerative colitis rests on a combination of clinical history, biochemical evidence of inflammation, stool studies that exclude infection, ileocolonoscopy with segmental biopsies, and supportive imaging. The 2019 ACG guideline (Rubin et al.) recommends starting with a thorough history covering stool frequency, blood, urgency, nocturnal symptoms, extraintestinal manifestations, NSAID use, and travel or antibiotic exposure. Initial laboratory work-up includes complete blood count, C-reactive protein, ESR, albumin, ferritin, and liver function tests — looking for anemia, hypoalbuminemia, and inflammatory signal. Stool studies are mandatory before treatment: stool culture, ova and parasites, and C. difficile PCR to exclude infectious colitis that can mimic or coexist with UC. Fecal calprotectin above 150-250 µg/g supports active mucosal inflammation and helps distinguish IBD from irritable bowel syndrome with high accuracy. The definitive diagnostic test is ileocolonoscopy with biopsies from each colonic segment plus the terminal ileum. UC shows continuous mucosal inflammation starting in the rectum and extending proximally with a clear demarcation, normal terminal ileum (except for occasional backwash ileitis in pancolitis), and histology demonstrating crypt distortion, crypt abscesses, basal plasmacytosis, and goblet cell depletion confined to the mucosa. Disease activity is scored using the Mayo endoscopic subscore (0 normal, 1 mild, 2 moderate, 3 severe) or the UCEIS. CT or MR enterography are reserved for distinguishing UC from Crohn's disease when small-bowel involvement is suspected. Flexible sigmoidoscopy without bowel preparation is preferred over full colonoscopy in suspected acute severe colitis to reduce perforation risk.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Gold-standard diagnostic test. Confirms continuous mucosal inflammation starting at the rectum, defines disease extent (Montreal E1/E2/E3), grades severity (Mayo endoscopic subscore, UCEIS), and excludes Crohn's disease and colorectal neoplasia. Histology shows crypt distortion, basal plasmacytosis, and goblet cell depletion.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Same-day procedure under sedation, lasting 20-40 minutes after a bowel preparation drink the day before. Patients fast from midnight, are sedated, and recover within 1-2 hours. Biopsy results typically return in 5-10 days.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Non-invasive marker of colonic inflammation. Useful for initial triage (distinguishing IBD from IBS), monitoring disease activity, and predicting flares. Levels above 150-250 µg/g support active inflammation; persistent normal values argue against a flare.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A small sample of stool collected at home in a provided container and returned to the lab. Results typically within 3-5 working days.

diagnosticTests.test: Stool studies for infection (culture, ova and parasites, C. difficile PCR)
diagnosticTests.purpose: Mandatory before starting immunosuppression and during any flare. Excludes infectious mimics — Salmonella, Shigella, Campylobacter, E. coli O157, amoebiasis — and superinfection with C. difficile, which complicates 5-10% of UC flares and worsens outcomes if missed.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: One or more stool samples collected at home and submitted to the lab. C. difficile PCR returns within 24 hours; culture takes 2-4 days.

diagnosticTests.test: Full blood count, CRP, ESR, ferritin, albumin, liver function
diagnosticTests.purpose: Detects anemia, systemic inflammatory activity, malnutrition, and primary sclerosing cholangitis. Trends guide severity assessment and response to therapy.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Standard blood draw; results return same day or next day. Repeated periodically through induction and maintenance.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Limited examination of the rectum and sigmoid colon. Preferred over full colonoscopy in acute severe colitis to reduce perforation risk, and useful for repeated disease activity assessment without bowel preparation or sedation.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Brief procedure, usually 10-15 minutes, often without sedation. Minor discomfort and gas during the exam are normal and resolve quickly.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Defines small-bowel involvement, helps distinguish UC from Crohn's, and identifies complications such as toxic megacolon, abscess, or strictures. MR is preferred when imaging is needed long-term to avoid radiation exposure.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Patient drinks 1-1.5 liters of oral contrast over 45-60 minutes before the scan. The scan itself takes 15-30 minutes. No sedation required.

diagnosticTests.test: Cytomegalovirus (CMV) testing on biopsy or blood
diagnosticTests.purpose: Performed during steroid-refractory or severe flares to identify CMV colitis, which complicates 20-30% of severe UC episodes and requires antiviral therapy with ganciclovir.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Either an additional histology stain on colon biopsies (immunohistochemistry) or a blood test (CMV PCR). Results available within 3-7 days.

diagnosticTests.test: MRCP and liver imaging when liver enzymes are elevated
diagnosticTests.purpose: Evaluates for primary sclerosing cholangitis, which affects roughly 5% of UC patients and changes long-term cancer surveillance strategy.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: MR cholangiopancreatography is a non-contrast 20-30 minute MRI of the bile ducts. No injections or sedation needed.

treatmentOverview: Treatment of ulcerative colitis is matched to disease extent and severity, then refined by treat-to-target follow-up. The 2019 ACG (Rubin et al., PMID 30840605) and 2020 AGA (Feuerstein et al., PMID 31816372) guidelines, together with the 2022 ECCO recommendations (Raine et al., PMID 35040476), structure care around three goals: induce clinical remission, achieve endoscopic healing (Mayo endoscopic subscore 0-1), and maintain steroid-free remission for life. STRIDE-II additionally endorses histologic remission where feasible. Mild-to-moderate proctitis is managed first with topical mesalamine — a 1 g suppository at bedtime — escalated to a mesalamine enema if proximal extension is suspected. Mild-to-moderate left-sided or extensive UC starts with oral 5-ASA at 2.4-4.8 g/day combined with topical mesalamine, with budesonide MMX 9 mg daily reserved as a steroid-sparing rescue when 5-ASA fails. Moderate-to-severe disease and steroid-dependent or refractory disease are escalated to one of several advanced therapy classes: anti-TNF agents (infliximab 5 mg/kg IV at weeks 0, 2, 6 then every 8 weeks; adalimumab 160/80/40 mg SC induction then every 2 weeks; golimumab 200/100/100 mg SC), anti-integrin vedolizumab 300 mg IV, IL-12/23 ustekinumab (6 mg/kg IV induction, then 90 mg SC every 8 weeks), IL-23p19 mirikizumab (LUCENT trial, Sandborn 2023 PMID 37133584) and risankizumab, JAK inhibitors (tofacitinib 10 mg twice daily induction, OCTAVE Sandborn 2017 PMID 28467869; upadacitinib 45 mg daily induction, ELEVATE UC Danese 2022 PMID 35644166), and S1P-receptor modulators (ozanimod 0.92 mg daily, TRUE NORTH Sands 2021 PMID 34081380; etrasimod 2 mg daily). Acute severe ulcerative colitis admitted to hospital is treated with IV methylprednisolone 60 mg per day; non-responders by day 3-5 receive rescue with infliximab 5-10 mg/kg or intravenous cyclosporine 2-4 mg/kg/day, with colectomy considered when medical therapy fails. Live vaccines must be given before immunosuppression begins; PPSV23/PCV20, recombinant zoster, annual influenza, COVID, and hepatitis B vaccinations are recommended prior to or during therapy. Surveillance colonoscopy starts eight years after diagnosis, then every 1-3 years according to risk (extent, PSC, family history, prior dysplasia).

medicalTreatments.name: Mesalamine suppositories (1 g at bedtime) or enemas (1-4 g nightly) — topical 5-ASA
medicalTreatments.description: First-line for ulcerative proctitis and proctosigmoiditis. Suppositories cover the distal 10-15 cm; enemas reach the splenic flexure. Combined with oral mesalamine for left-sided or extensive disease for additive benefit.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Clinical remission in 60-70% of mild-to-moderate proctitis within 4-8 weeks (ACG 2019).

medicalTreatments.name: Oral mesalamine (2.4-4.8 g daily) — systemic 5-ASA
medicalTreatments.description: Mainstay of induction and maintenance therapy in mild-to-moderate extensive UC. Once-daily dosing is as effective as divided doses and improves adherence. Doses above 2.4 g/day produce higher remission rates without added toxicity.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Clinical remission in roughly 40-55% at 8 weeks and maintenance of remission in 50-70% at one year when used at adequate dose.

medicalTreatments.name: Budesonide MMX (9 mg daily for 8 weeks)
medicalTreatments.description: Multimatrix oral steroid with high first-pass hepatic metabolism, delivering steroid effect to the colonic mucosa with limited systemic exposure. Used when 5-ASA induction fails but advanced therapy is not yet indicated.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Clinical and endoscopic remission in roughly 18-20% by week 8 versus 7-8% with placebo (CORE trials); a steroid-sparing bridging option.

medicalTreatments.name: Systemic corticosteroids (oral prednisolone 40-60 mg daily for moderate-severe flare; IV methylprednisolone 60 mg daily for ASUC)
medicalTreatments.description: Used to induce — never maintain — remission. Tapered over 6-8 weeks once a flare is controlled. Failure to taper, or relapse on taper, defines steroid dependence and triggers escalation to biologic or small-molecule therapy.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Roughly 70% of patients respond within 5-7 days; about 30% fail IV steroids in ASUC and require rescue or colectomy.

medicalTreatments.name: Infliximab (5 mg/kg IV at weeks 0, 2, 6 then every 8 weeks) — anti-TNF
medicalTreatments.description: Anti-TNF monoclonal antibody. First biologic approved for UC. Effective for moderate-severe disease and for IV rescue in steroid-refractory ASUC. Test for latent TB and hepatitis B prior to starting.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Clinical remission in 33-39% and mucosal healing in roughly 60% at week 8 in ACT-1 and ACT-2 trials. About 50% of responders maintain remission at 1 year.

medicalTreatments.name: Adalimumab (160 mg, then 80 mg, then 40 mg every 2 weeks SC) — anti-TNF
medicalTreatments.description: Subcutaneous TNF inhibitor; convenient home administration. Lower remission rates than infliximab in indirect comparisons but acceptable when self-injection is preferred.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Clinical remission in roughly 17% at week 8 (ULTRA trials) and 8% sustained at week 52 in the more conservative endpoints.

medicalTreatments.name: Golimumab (200 mg then 100 mg at week 2; 100 mg every 4 weeks SC) — anti-TNF
medicalTreatments.description: Subcutaneous TNF inhibitor approved on the basis of the PURSUIT trials. Reasonable second anti-TNF option after adalimumab failure when infliximab is impractical.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Clinical response in roughly 50% and remission in about 18% at week 6 in PURSUIT-SC.

medicalTreatments.name: Vedolizumab (300 mg IV at weeks 0, 2, 6 then every 8 weeks) — anti-integrin
medicalTreatments.description: Selective gut-tropic α4β7 integrin antagonist. Excellent long-term safety profile; favored in older patients, those with infection risk, or after anti-TNF failure. GEMINI-1 trial supports both induction and maintenance.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Clinical remission in roughly 17% at week 6 induction and 42% maintenance at 52 weeks (GEMINI-1, biologic-naive subgroup higher).

medicalTreatments.name: Ustekinumab (6 mg/kg IV induction, then 90 mg SC every 8 weeks) — IL-12/23
medicalTreatments.description: Monoclonal antibody against the shared p40 subunit of IL-12 and IL-23. Effective in both biologic-naive and biologic-experienced patients per UNIFI trial. Favorable safety profile.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Clinical remission in 15-19% at week 8 induction and roughly 43% at week 44 in UNIFI.

medicalTreatments.name: Mirikizumab (300 mg IV q4wk induction × 3 doses, then 200 mg SC every 4 weeks) — IL-23p19
medicalTreatments.description: First IL-23 p19-selective antibody approved for UC. Pivotal LUCENT-1 and LUCENT-2 trials demonstrated induction and maintenance efficacy in biologic-naive and biologic-experienced patients (Sandborn 2023, PMID 37133584).
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Clinical remission in roughly 24% at week 12 and 50% at week 52 among week-12 responders (LUCENT).

medicalTreatments.name: Risankizumab (1200 mg IV induction at weeks 0, 4, 8, then 180-360 mg SC every 8 weeks) — IL-23p19
medicalTreatments.description: Second IL-23 p19 antibody approved for UC, supported by the INSPIRE and COMMAND trials. Reasonable option after anti-TNF failure or as a first-line advanced therapy.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Clinical remission in roughly 20-21% at induction and 40% at maintenance week 52 in pivotal trials.

medicalTreatments.name: Tofacitinib (10 mg twice daily for 8 weeks induction; 5-10 mg twice daily maintenance) — pan-JAK inhibitor
medicalTreatments.description: Oral small molecule with rapid onset; effective in biologic-experienced patients. FDA boxed warning following ORAL Surveillance signaled higher rates of major adverse cardiovascular events, thromboembolism, and malignancy versus TNF inhibitors in rheumatoid arthritis populations — caution in patients over 65, smokers, and those with cardiovascular risk factors (Sandborn OCTAVE, NEJM 2017, PMID 28467869).
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Clinical remission in 16-18% at week 8 induction and 34-41% at week 52 maintenance (OCTAVE Induction and Sustain).

medicalTreatments.name: Upadacitinib (45 mg daily for 8 weeks induction; 15-30 mg daily maintenance) — JAK1-selective
medicalTreatments.description: Selective JAK1 inhibitor with rapid onset. ELEVATE UC 52 and ELEVATE UC 12 demonstrated higher efficacy than placebo across biologic-naive and biologic-experienced patients (Danese 2022, PMID 35644166). Shares the JAK-class warnings, applied with caution in older or higher-risk patients.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Clinical remission in 26-33% at week 8 induction and roughly 42-52% at week 52 maintenance in ELEVATE.

medicalTreatments.name: Ozanimod (0.92 mg once daily after titration) — S1P receptor modulator
medicalTreatments.description: Oral sphingosine-1-phosphate receptor modulator that sequesters lymphocytes in lymph nodes. TRUE NORTH supported induction and maintenance efficacy with a manageable safety profile (Sands 2021, PMID 34081380). Baseline ophthalmology and cardiology screening recommended.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Clinical remission in 18% at week 10 induction and 37% at week 52 maintenance (TRUE NORTH).

medicalTreatments.name: Etrasimod (2 mg once daily) — S1P receptor modulator
medicalTreatments.description: Second oral S1P modulator approved for UC on the basis of the ELEVATE UC 52 and ELEVATE UC 12 trials. Convenient once-daily oral dosing without injection.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Clinical remission in 27% at week 12 and 32% at week 52 in pivotal trials.

medicalTreatments.name: Cyclosporine (2-4 mg/kg/day IV for steroid-refractory ASUC)
medicalTreatments.description: Calcineurin inhibitor used as IV rescue therapy in ASUC at day 3-5 if response to steroids fails. Requires close monitoring of blood pressure, magnesium, renal function, and drug levels. Bridge to maintenance with thiopurine or biologic.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Short-term response rate of roughly 70-80% in ASUC, with about 50% colectomy-free at 1 year.

surgicalOptions.name: Total proctocolectomy with ileal pouch-anal anastomosis (J-pouch IPAA)
surgicalOptions.description: Definitive curative procedure. Removes the entire colon and rectum, creates a pouch from terminal ileum, and reconnects to the anal canal — usually in two or three stages with a temporary diverting ileostomy. Performed for medically refractory disease, dysplasia, or cancer.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Functional pouch in 85-95% at 10 years; pouchitis develops in roughly 50% over time and is usually treatable with antibiotics.

surgicalOptions.name: Total proctocolectomy with end ileostomy
surgicalOptions.description: Removes the colon, rectum, and anus with a permanent ileostomy. Considered when pouch surgery is unsuitable (older age, sphincter dysfunction, advanced cancer, or patient preference).
surgicalOptions.successRate: Operative mortality under 1%; long-term satisfaction with stoma is high in selected patients.

surgicalOptions.name: Subtotal colectomy with end ileostomy (emergency)
surgicalOptions.description: Performed for acute severe colitis with toxic megacolon, perforation, or steroid- and rescue-refractory disease. The rectum is preserved temporarily, allowing later pouch surgery if appropriate.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Lifesaving in fulminant disease; 80-85% subsequently undergo elective completion proctectomy with pouch reconstruction.

alternativeTreatments.name: Curcumin (turmeric extract, 2-3 g daily)
alternativeTreatments.description: Several randomized trials (notably Lang 2015) support curcumin as adjunctive therapy to mesalamine, with higher remission rates than mesalamine alone in mild-to-moderate UC. Use a standardized preparation rather than dietary turmeric.

alternativeTreatments.name: Fecal microbiota transplantation (FMT)
alternativeTreatments.description: Investigational for mild-to-moderate UC; multiple RCTs (Moayyedi 2015, Paramsothy 2017) showed modestly higher remission rates than placebo. Not yet a standard-of-care option outside research protocols or recurrent C. difficile co-infection.

alternativeTreatments.name: Mind-body therapies (CBT, gut-directed hypnotherapy)
alternativeTreatments.description: Adjunctive support for fatigue, anxiety, and pain perception in stable UC. Do not affect mucosal inflammation but improve quality-of-life scores in trials.

whySeeSpecialist: A gastroenterologist should lead care from the point of initial diagnostic colonoscopy onward. Specialist input is essential for confirming UC versus Crohn's, choosing between 5-ASA and advanced therapy, managing treatment failures, running inpatient care for acute severe colitis, coordinating colorectal surgery, and overseeing long-term dysplasia surveillance. Hepatology, rheumatology, dermatology, and ophthalmology are added when extraintestinal manifestations occur.
doctorSelectionGuide: Look for a board-certified gastroenterologist with experience in inflammatory bowel disease — ideally one practicing within an IBD-focused unit with infusion capacity, on-site advanced endoscopy, dedicated IBD nursing, and rapid access to a colorectal surgeon experienced in pouch surgery. Ask whether the practice uses treat-to-target follow-up with endoscopy and calprotectin, and how rapidly they can admit patients in a flare. Continuity matters — UC is a multi-decade relationship.
preventionStrategies: There is no proven primary prevention for ulcerative colitis itself; focus instead on early detection — investigate persistent bloody diarrhea promptly rather than attributing it to hemorrhoids
Maintain endoscopic remission (Mayo subscore 0-1) on appropriate maintenance therapy — the strongest determinant of long-term outcomes
Adhere to long-term 5-ASA in mild disease — chronic mesalamine reduces colorectal cancer risk by roughly 50% in observational studies
Complete recommended vaccinations (PPSV23/PCV20, recombinant zoster, annual influenza, COVID, hepatitis B) before starting immunosuppression
Attend surveillance colonoscopy from 8 years after diagnosis, then every 1-3 years depending on extent, PSC status, and family history
Avoid non-essential NSAIDs, which can precipitate flares; use paracetamol or short-course topical agents as preferred analgesics
lifestyleModifications: Follow a Mediterranean-style eating pattern rich in vegetables, fruits, fish, olive oil, and whole grains; limit ultra-processed foods, refined sugar, and red meat
Identify and reduce personal trigger foods through a food and symptom diary rather than blanket restriction — there is no single UC diet
Maintain hydration during flares with electrolyte solutions to offset losses from frequent stools
Stop smoking; the long-term cardiovascular and cancer benefits outweigh any short-term UC effect, and nicotine cessation should be combined with proactive medical optimization
Limit alcohol intake — heavy use is associated with relapse and complicates medication metabolism, particularly with JAK inhibitors and S1P modulators
Build regular low-to-moderate intensity aerobic activity (walking, cycling, swimming) into the week to reduce systemic inflammation and improve mood
Prioritize sleep and stress management — both are independent triggers of clinical relapse in IBD cohorts

recommended: Cooked vegetables, peeled fruits, and well-tolerated fiber sources during stable disease
Lean protein from fish, poultry, eggs, and low-fat dairy as tolerated
Omega-3-rich foods (oily fish such as salmon, mackerel, sardines) two to three times weekly
Adequate iron, calcium, and vitamin D — most UC patients benefit from supplementation, especially during flares
Hydration with water, clear soups, and oral rehydration solutions during flares
Mediterranean-pattern foods overall, with olive oil as the primary added fat
avoid: Ultra-processed foods, fast food, and items high in emulsifiers (polysorbate-80, carboxymethylcellulose) where possible
High-residue raw vegetables and whole nuts during active flares — reintroduce when symptoms settle
High-fructose corn syrup and sugar-sweetened beverages, which worsen diarrhea and are linked epidemiologically with IBD risk
Excess red and processed meat (limit to one to two servings per week)
Alcohol during active flares; moderate use only in stable remission
Routine NSAID use for pain (ibuprofen, naproxen, diclofenac), which can precipitate flares

exerciseGuidelines: Regular moderate aerobic exercise — 30 minutes most days — is safe and beneficial in stable UC, reducing fatigue and improving quality of life. During an acute flare, rest as needed and resume activity gradually as bowel symptoms settle. Resistance training helps offset steroid-related muscle loss and bone density decline. Avoid high-intensity training during severe flares due to dehydration and electrolyte risk.
dailyManagement: Take maintenance medication at the same time daily — adherence to 5-ASA below 80% roughly doubles relapse risk
Track stool frequency, blood, urgency, and any new pain in a simple journal or app to detect early relapse
Monitor fecal calprotectin every 3-6 months in stable disease, and within 4-6 weeks of any treatment change
Stay current on vaccinations and update your gastroenterologist before starting any new medication, especially antibiotics or NSAIDs
Carry a medication card listing your current biologic or small-molecule therapy, last infusion date, and contact details for your IBD team
Plan travel with extra medication, a copy of your treatment summary, and access to a referral clinic at the destination
prognosis: With consistent treat-to-target management, modern outcomes for ulcerative colitis are markedly better than a generation ago. About 50% of patients achieve sustained steroid-free remission on first-line 5-ASA, and most of the remainder reach durable remission on a biologic, JAK inhibitor, or S1P modulator. Lifetime colectomy rates have fallen from over 30% in the pre-biologic era to roughly 10-15% in contemporary cohorts. Endoscopic and histologic healing, once achieved, predicts the longest flare-free intervals and the lowest cumulative colorectal cancer risk. Cancer risk rises after eight years of disease and accumulates by roughly 1% per year of duration after the first decade in patients with extensive disease, but is sharply reduced by adherent maintenance therapy and structured surveillance. Patients with primary sclerosing cholangitis carry the highest cancer risk and need annual colonoscopy. Overall life expectancy in well-managed UC is close to that of the general population, and most patients work, parent, and travel without significant restriction.
recoveryTimeline: Mild flares typically settle over 2-4 weeks with topical or oral 5-ASA. Moderate flares treated with oral steroids show meaningful symptom improvement within 5-7 days, with steroids tapered over 6-8 weeks. Biologic and small-molecule induction works on different schedules: JAK inhibitors and S1P modulators show response within 1-2 weeks; anti-TNF and ustekinumab over 4-8 weeks; vedolizumab and IL-23 agents over 8-14 weeks. Acute severe ulcerative colitis admitted to hospital typically responds to IV steroids within 3 days; non-responders are escalated to infliximab or cyclosporine at day 3-5 with response assessed within 5-7 days. After J-pouch surgery, full pouch adaptation takes 6-12 months, during which stool frequency gradually falls from 10-15 to 4-6 per day.
complications: Acute severe ulcerative colitis (ASUC) — admission for IV steroids and rescue therapy with infliximab or cyclosporine; colectomy if medical therapy fails
Toxic megacolon — colonic dilation greater than 6 cm with systemic toxicity; surgical emergency with high perforation risk
Colonic perforation — typically in the setting of toxic megacolon or severe deep ulceration; carries mortality up to 30% if delayed
Venous thromboembolism — DVT and pulmonary embolism risk is roughly three-fold elevated in active UC; prophylactic anticoagulation is recommended for hospitalized patients
Colorectal cancer — cumulative risk roughly 1% per year beyond 10 years of disease in extensive UC, modified by extent, PSC, family history, and dysplasia
Iron-deficiency and anemia of chronic disease — common, undertreated, and a major driver of fatigue
Primary sclerosing cholangitis — affects roughly 5% of UC patients; raises colorectal cancer and cholangiocarcinoma risk and warrants annual colonoscopy
Pouchitis after J-pouch — develops in roughly 50% over time; usually responds to antibiotics, occasionally requires biologic therapy or pouch revision
Growth failure and delayed puberty in pediatric-onset UC — drives early consideration of escalation to biologics

supportResources.name: Crohn's & Colitis Foundation (USA)
supportResources.description: Authoritative US patient organization with disease education, peer support, and clinical trial directories.

supportResources.name: Crohn's & Colitis UK
supportResources.description: UK charity offering patient guides, helpline, and IBD passports for travel and emergencies.

supportResources.name: European Crohn's and Colitis Organisation (ECCO)
supportResources.description: European clinical society publishing the ECCO guidelines used across European and Asian IBD care.

supportResources.name: American College of Gastroenterology — UC Patient Information
supportResources.description: US specialty society resource aligned with the 2019 ACG ulcerative colitis guideline.

confusedWithConditions.name: Crohn's Disease
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Crohn's disease causes transmural inflammation that can affect any part of the gastrointestinal tract from mouth to anus, with skip lesions, granulomas, fistulas, and perianal involvement. UC is confined to the colon with continuous mucosal inflammation from the rectum proximally, no skip lesions, and no fistulas. Smoking worsens Crohn's but is paradoxically protective in UC.

confusedWithConditions.name: Irritable Bowel Syndrome
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: IBS produces cramping, bloating, and altered bowel habits without blood, weight loss, anemia, nocturnal symptoms, or systemic inflammation. Fecal calprotectin is normal in IBS and elevated in active UC. Colonoscopy is normal in IBS and abnormal in UC.

confusedWithConditions.name: Microscopic Colitis
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Microscopic colitis causes chronic non-bloody watery diarrhea with a normal-appearing colon on endoscopy; diagnosis requires biopsies showing lymphocytic or collagenous infiltration. UC produces visible ulceration and bloody stools.

confusedWithConditions.name: Clostridioides difficile Colitis
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: C. difficile colitis is an acute infectious colitis typically following antibiotic exposure, with positive C. difficile PCR or toxin. It can mimic or complicate UC; testing for C. difficile is mandatory in any UC flare.

confusedWithConditions.name: Infectious Colitis
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Acute bacterial or parasitic colitis (Salmonella, Shigella, Campylobacter, amoebiasis) presents with bloody diarrhea over days, often with travel or food exposure history. Stool culture identifies the pathogen; symptoms typically resolve within 1-2 weeks, unlike the protracted course of UC.

relatedConditions.name: Crohn's Disease
relatedConditions.relevance: The other major inflammatory bowel disease; shares pathophysiology, many treatments, and surveillance principles, but differs in anatomy and surgical strategy.

relatedConditions.name: Colorectal Cancer
relatedConditions.relevance: Cumulative cancer risk rises with disease duration and extent; surveillance colonoscopy starts 8 years after UC diagnosis.

relatedConditions.name: Irritable Bowel Syndrome
relatedConditions.relevance: Functional gut symptoms frequently overlay UC in remission; distinguishing IBS-like symptoms from true inflammation guides escalation decisions and avoids unnecessary therapy changes.

relatedConditions.name: Anemia
relatedConditions.relevance: Iron-deficiency and anemia of chronic disease are common, undertreated, and a major driver of fatigue in UC.

faqs.question: Is ulcerative colitis curable?
faqs.answer: Ulcerative colitis is not curable with medication; the underlying immune dysregulation persists. However, modern therapies induce durable remission in most patients, and total proctocolectomy with ileal pouch-anal anastomosis is curative for the disease itself, though it brings its own long-term considerations such as pouchitis. With consistent treat-to-target management, lifetime colectomy rates are 10-15%.

faqs.question: What is the difference between ulcerative colitis and Crohn's disease?
faqs.answer: Ulcerative colitis affects only the colon with continuous mucosal inflammation starting at the rectum, while Crohn's disease can affect any part of the gut from mouth to anus, with skip lesions, transmural inflammation, and a tendency to form fistulas and strictures. UC produces bloody diarrhea; Crohn's more often causes abdominal pain, weight loss, and perianal disease. Smoking worsens Crohn's but is protective in UC.

faqs.question: What does an ulcerative colitis flare feel like?
faqs.answer: A flare typically brings bloody loose stools several times per day, urgency that disrupts work and sleep, lower abdominal cramping that eases briefly after passing stool, and persistent fatigue. In severe flares, fever, racing heart, and weight loss develop, signaling acute severe colitis that needs hospital admission. Most flares build over days to a few weeks.

faqs.question: How is ulcerative colitis diagnosed?
faqs.answer: Diagnosis combines history of bloody diarrhea, blood tests showing inflammation or anemia, stool tests to exclude infection, fecal calprotectin, and an ileocolonoscopy with biopsies that demonstrates continuous mucosal inflammation starting in the rectum. Histology confirms crypt distortion, crypt abscesses, and basal plasmacytosis confined to the mucosa.

faqs.question: What is the best treatment for mild ulcerative colitis?
faqs.answer: Mild proctitis usually responds to a 1 g mesalamine suppository at bedtime, sometimes combined with a mesalamine enema. Mild left-sided or extensive disease is treated with oral mesalamine at 2.4-4.8 g/day plus topical mesalamine. Once-daily dosing is as effective as divided doses. Treatment is continued long-term to maintain remission.

faqs.question: What are biologics for ulcerative colitis?
faqs.answer: Biologics are injectable or infused antibodies that block specific inflammatory pathways. Options include anti-TNF agents (infliximab, adalimumab, golimumab), anti-integrin vedolizumab, IL-12/23 ustekinumab, and IL-23p19 agents mirikizumab and risankizumab. They are used for moderate-to-severe disease or steroid-dependent or steroid-refractory disease, with remission rates ranging from 15-35% at week 8 across trials.

faqs.question: Can ulcerative colitis turn into colon cancer?
faqs.answer: Long-standing extensive ulcerative colitis increases colorectal cancer risk, with cumulative incidence rising roughly 1% per year of disease beyond the first decade. Risk is higher with extensive disease, primary sclerosing cholangitis, family history, and persistent inflammation. Surveillance colonoscopy starting 8 years after diagnosis, with chromoendoscopy or high-definition white light, substantially reduces cancer mortality.

faqs.question: Will I need surgery for ulcerative colitis?
faqs.answer: Lifetime colectomy rates have fallen to 10-15% with modern medical therapy. Surgery is recommended for medically refractory disease, severe flares unresponsive to rescue therapy, dysplasia or cancer, and selected complications such as toxic megacolon or perforation. The most common operation is total proctocolectomy with ileal pouch-anal anastomosis (J-pouch), usually in two or three stages.

faqs.question: What is a J-pouch?
faqs.answer: A J-pouch is a reservoir created from the terminal ileum after total proctocolectomy that is connected to the anal canal, allowing bowel movements through the natural anus rather than a permanent stoma. Most patients pass stool 4-6 times per day once the pouch adapts over 6-12 months. Pouchitis develops in roughly 50% over time and is usually treatable with antibiotics.

faqs.question: What diet should I follow with ulcerative colitis?
faqs.answer: No single diet treats ulcerative colitis. A Mediterranean-style pattern with fish, olive oil, whole grains, and cooked vegetables is supported by observational and trial data. Limit ultra-processed foods, sugar-sweetened beverages, and excess red meat. During flares, soft low-residue foods are usually better tolerated. A registered dietitian familiar with IBD can personalize the plan.

faqs.question: Is ulcerative colitis hereditary?
faqs.answer: There is a clear genetic component. First-degree relatives have a 3-15% lifetime risk compared with around 0.3% in the general population. Over 200 risk loci have been identified, but no single test predicts disease. Most patients have no affected family member at the time of diagnosis.

faqs.question: Can children get ulcerative colitis?
faqs.answer: Yes. About 20% of UC patients are diagnosed before age 18, with peak pediatric incidence around 10-15 years. Children more often present with extensive colitis, growth failure, and delayed puberty, and pediatric guidelines support earlier escalation to biologic therapy. Long-term outcomes are excellent with specialist pediatric IBD care.

faqs.question: Can women with ulcerative colitis have a healthy pregnancy?
faqs.answer: Yes. Conception during remission gives the best pregnancy outcomes; most maintenance therapies (mesalamine, thiopurines, anti-TNF agents, ustekinumab, vedolizumab) are continued through pregnancy. JAK inhibitors and methotrexate are avoided. Flares during pregnancy are riskier than treatment exposure. Preconception counseling with the IBD team is the most important step.

faqs.question: How quickly does treatment work?
faqs.answer: Topical and oral 5-ASA work over 2-4 weeks. Oral steroids show meaningful symptom improvement within 5-7 days. JAK inhibitors and S1P modulators respond within 1-2 weeks. Anti-TNF and ustekinumab work over 4-8 weeks; vedolizumab and IL-23 agents over 8-14 weeks. Endoscopic healing typically lags behind symptomatic response.

faqs.question: What is acute severe ulcerative colitis?
faqs.answer: Acute severe ulcerative colitis (ASUC) is defined by Truelove-Witts criteria — more than six bloody stools per day plus tachycardia, fever, anemia, or elevated ESR. It requires same-day hospital admission, IV methylprednisolone, prophylactic anticoagulation, and structured rescue planning with infliximab or cyclosporine at day 3-5 if steroids fail. Roughly 30% require colectomy during the admission.

faqs.question: Should I stop smoking if I have ulcerative colitis?
faqs.answer: Yes. Although active smoking is statistically associated with milder UC, the cardiovascular, cancer, and respiratory harms of smoking far outweigh any potential benefit. Smokers should quit while their IBD team optimizes maintenance therapy to offset any short-term flare risk after cessation.

faqs.question: How often should I have a colonoscopy with ulcerative colitis?
faqs.answer: Surveillance colonoscopy is recommended starting 8 years after diagnosis for left-sided or extensive disease, then every 1-3 years depending on risk factors. Patients with primary sclerosing cholangitis need annual colonoscopy from the time both diagnoses are established. Surveillance is usually performed with chromoendoscopy or high-definition white light with targeted biopsies.

faqs.question: Can ulcerative colitis affect other parts of the body?
faqs.answer: Yes. Extraintestinal manifestations occur in 25-40% of patients and include peripheral arthritis, axial spondyloarthritis, episcleritis or uveitis, erythema nodosum, pyoderma gangrenosum, and primary sclerosing cholangitis (PSC, ~5%). Some, such as peripheral arthritis, track with bowel activity; others, such as PSC and axial spondyloarthritis, have an independent course.

faqs.question: Do I need vaccinations before starting immunosuppression?
faqs.answer: Yes. Before starting biologics, JAK inhibitors, or steroids, vaccinations should be updated: pneumococcal (PPSV23/PCV20), recombinant zoster, annual influenza, COVID-19, hepatitis B, and HPV where age-appropriate. Live vaccines must be completed before immunosuppression begins. Latent tuberculosis and hepatitis B screening are mandatory prior to anti-TNF therapy.

faqs.question: How much does ulcerative colitis treatment cost?
faqs.answer: Generic mesalamine and corticosteroids are inexpensive and widely available, often a few US dollars per month. Biologics and small molecules vary widely — list prices range from several hundred to several thousand dollars per dose, but most patients access them through insurance, biosimilars, or manufacturer support programs. In India and other emerging markets, biosimilar infliximab and adalimumab have substantially lowered access barriers.

faqs.question: Will I have ulcerative colitis for life?
faqs.answer: Yes, the underlying immune tendency persists for life, though most patients spend most of their time in remission with appropriate maintenance therapy. Colectomy removes the colon and eliminates UC-specific inflammation, although the pouch and rectal cuff can still become inflamed. With modern treat-to-target care, life expectancy and quality of life are close to that of the general population.

metaDescription: Ulcerative colitis: bloody diarrhea, urgency, tenesmus from continuous colon inflammation. Symptoms, diagnosis, biologics, and surgery reviewed by our team.
